title: System Design Fundamentals
description: Core concepts for distributed systems design — CAP theorem, consistent hashing, caching strategies, sharding patterns, consensus algorithms, and trade-off analysis. Covers the mental models every FAANG interview expects.
details:
FAANG Interview Pattern: System design rounds follow a predictable structure. The interviewer evaluates: (1) Requirements gathering — functional vs non-functional, (2) Capacity estimation — DAU, QPS, storage, bandwidth, (3) Data model — SQL vs NoSQL, schema, indexing, (4) High-level design — components, data flow, (5) Deep dive — the interesting tradeoff (consistency model, sharding strategy, caching layer), (6) Bottleneck analysis — what breaks at 10x/100x scale, (7) Trade-off summary — what you chose and why. The 7 topics in this file cover the cross-cutting patterns that appear in every design round.

Key principle: There is no perfect design — only tradeoffs. Every FAANG interview tests your ability to identify the right tradeoff for the given constraints. Always state the tradeoff explicitly: "I'm choosing CP over AP because financial transactions require strong consistency, and I'll handle the brief unavailability with client-side retry + exponential backoff." The best candidates discuss 2-3 alternatives before committing to one.

Company-specific emphasis: Google focuses on planet-scale services and distributed systems depth. Meta emphasizes data stores (social graph sharding), caching, and real-time feeds. Amazon stresses loosely-coupled services, fault tolerance, and cost-efficiency. Apple focuses on privacy-preserving designs and end-to-end latency for consumer devices.

sections.title: CAP Theorem — Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance
sections.description:
The CAP theorem (Brewer, 2000; proved by Gilbert & Lynch, 2002) states that a distributed data store can only guarantee two of three properties simultaneously during a network partition:

  • Consistency (C): Every read receives the most recent write or an error. Formalized as linearizability — operations appear atomic at a single point between invocation and response. read(x) → latest_write(x).
  • Availability (A): Every non-failing node returns a response for every request. Not the latest data is allowed; hanging or erroring is not.
  • Partition Tolerance (P): The system continues functioning despite arbitrary message loss or network partition. Partitions are unavoidable — network splits, packet loss, GC pauses, cross-region latency.

The key insight: During a network partition, P is forced — you must choose between C and A. This is not a static label; most systems behave differently in normal operation vs partition mode.

PACELC Extension (Abadi, 2010): If Partition (P) → tradeoff A vs C. Else (E), even without partitions, there is a tradeoff between Latency (L) and Consistency (C). Synchronous replication (write_concern=majority) increases latency but guarantees consistency. Asynchronous replication is faster but risks stale reads. This reframes CAP as a continuous design spectrum, not just a failure-mode constraint.

Real-world classification:

  • ZooKeeper / Etcd (CP): Zab / Raft consensus. During leader election, the system is unavailable until a new leader is elected. Guarantees linearizable consistency at the cost of brief unavailability (200ms for a 5-node ZooKeeper ensemble).
  • Cassandra / DynamoDB (AP): Always accept writes. Conflicts resolved via last-write-wins (LWW), CRDTs, or application-level merge. DynamoDB: eventually consistent reads by default; strongly consistent reads at higher latency (quorum reads).
  • MongoDB (CP-default, configurable): Replica sets default to primary reads (strong consistency). writeConcern: majority for committed durability. Secondary reads available for eventual consistency (AP-like during partition).
  • Kafka (AP): Even with acks=all + min.insync.replicas, Kafka prioritizes availability — old leaders that lose contact with ZooKeeper step down, and the new leader may be missing uncommitted data.

sections.title: Consistent Hashing — Ring-Based Distribution
sections.description:
Problem with hash(key) % N: Adding or removing a node remaps almost all keys. For N=100, removing 1 node remaps 99% of keys → cache miss storm that can take down the database.

Consistent Hashing (Karger et al., 1997): Keys and nodes are hashed onto a circular ring [0, 2m-1] (m=32 or 64). A key is assigned to the nearest node clockwise on the ring. When a node is added or removed, only keys in its immediate arc are remapped — expected K / N keys move (K = total keys, N = total nodes) vs O(K) for modulo.

Virtual nodes (vnodes): Each physical node is replicated into R virtual nodes at random ring positions (e.g., R=150). Benefits: (1) Even load distribution — avoids skew when a node holds a hot arc. (2) Heterogeneous capacity — powerful nodes get more vnodes. (3) Failure redistribution — a dead node's load is spread across all survivors, not just one clockwise neighbor. Used by Dynamo, Cassandra, Riak, ScyllaDB.

The hot key problem: A single viral key can overload the node responsible for it. Mitigations: (1) Replicate the hot key and read from any replica. (2) Client-side cache the hot key to absorb reads. (3) Detect and rebalance dynamically (Cassandra's dynamic snitch + nodetool move).

Jump Consistent Hashing (Lamping & Veach, Google, 2014): An O(log n) algorithm that assigns keys to buckets without a ring or virtual nodes. Zero skew, no memory overhead. Tradeoff: Only supports adding nodes to the end of the list — cannot remove arbitrary nodes. Formula: b = jch(hash(key), n). Used by Google's internal Memcache.

sections.title: Caching Strategies — Eviction, Write Policies, and Architecture
sections.description:
Caching is the #1 performance optimization in distributed systems. FAANG interviews expect you to compare eviction policies, write strategies, and cache architectures with tradeoffs.

Eviction Policies:

  • LRU (Least Recently Used): Evict the item accessed longest ago. O(1) via doubly-linked list + hashmap. Weakness: Not scan-resistant — a single scan of 1M new items pollutes the entire cache. Mitigation: LRU-K (track last K access times) or 2Q (two queues: one-time and frequent).
  • LFU (Least Frequently Used): Evict least-frequently-accessed items. Better for skewed access patterns. Weakness: Items that were once popular linger forever (frequency decay). Mitigation: TinyLFU (Caffeine) — Bloom-filter frequency sketch with exponential decay, near-optimal hit rate at O(1) overhead.
  • FIFO: Evict oldest item. O(1), no metadata. Rarely used alone. Useful combined with second-chance (clock algorithm).
  • ARC (Adaptive Replacement Cache): IBM research — dynamically balances LRU and LFU via an adaptive tuning parameter. Used by PostgreSQL, ZFS, Varnish. Best general-purpose choice for unknown workloads.

Write Strategies:

  • Write-through: Write to cache AND DB synchronously. Ensures consistency, doubles write latency. Use for: read-heavy with strong consistency.
  • Write-behind (write-back): Write to cache, async flush to DB. Lowers write latency, risks data loss if cache fails. Use for: write-heavy, high throughput, eventual consistency OK.
  • Write-around: Write directly to DB, populate cache only on read. Avoids polluting cache with data never read.

Cache Patterns:

  • Cache-aside (lazy loading): App checks cache → miss → read DB → populate cache. Problem: cache stampede — N concurrent requests all hit DB simultaneously. Mitigation: mutex (one thread reloads), probabilistic early expiration (XFetch, Fan et al. 2015), or lease reads (Memcached).
  • Read-through: Cache layer auto-populates from DB on miss. Simpler app code.
  • Write-through + Read-through: Fully managed cache (Redis Enterprise, ElastiCache, Azure Redis).

Redis vs Memcached:

  • Redis: Rich types (strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams), persistence (RDB snapshots, AOF log), replication (master-replica), Lua scripting, pub/sub, transactions. Single-threaded core (I/O threads in 6.x+). Use: leaderboards, rate limiters, session stores, job queues, distributed locks (Redlock).
  • Memcached: Simple key-value, multi-threaded (4+ cores), no persistence, no replication. Lower per-op overhead. Use: simple query result caching, HTML fragment caching, session storage (when persistence not needed).

CDN Caching: Browser cache → CDN edge (POP) → CDN regional → origin. Each layer has TTL. Invalidation: (1) TTL expiry, (2) versioned URLs (app.css?v=2), (3) cache purge API (slow, global propagation), (4) cache tags (purge by tag, e.g., user:42). Edge compute (CloudFlare Workers, Lambda@Edge) allows dynamic assembly at the edge without origin round-trips.

sections.title: Database Sharding — Horizontal Partitioning Strategies
sections.description:
Horizontal partitioning (sharding) splits a database across multiple machines to overcome single-node limits (storage, CPU, I/O, memory). FAANG interviews expect deep knowledge of sharding strategies and their tradeoffs.

Sharding Strategies:

  • Hash-based sharding: shard = hash(key) % N. Even distribution, minimal hot spots. Problem: Adding/removing nodes remaps all keys (without consistent hashing). Use when: simple, evenly-distributed keys, no range queries.
  • Range-based sharding: Partition by key range (users 0000-1FFF → shard 1, 2000-3FFF → shard 2). Efficient for range scans. Problems: Hot spots at range edges, uneven distribution, range rebalancing requires moving large chunks.
  • Directory-based sharding: Lookup service maps keys → shards. Full flexibility. Problems: Single point of failure, additional latency per query, directory bottleneck. Used by: Elasticsearch (shard routing), HDFS NameNode.

Consistent hashing for sharding: The ring-based approach (Section 2) is the dominant strategy for NoSQL databases (Cassandra, Riak, Scylla). Composite sharding keys: Use multiple columns ((tenant_id, user_id)) to co-locate related data on the same shard while distributing across tenants.

Rebalancing challenges (the hardest problem in sharding):

  • Moving data is expensive: A 500GB shard migration saturates network bandwidth for hours.
  • Incremental migration: Cassandra uses token ranges — adding a node splits ranges; data is streamed incrementally. Virtual nodes make this fine-grained.
  • Logical shards (pre-sharding): Create more shards than physical nodes (e.g., 1024 logical shards on 8 physical nodes). Scale by reassigning logical shards. Used by: Vitess (MySQL sharding), ScyllaDB.
  • Re-sharding: When you outgrow the shard count, you must re-shard with more shards. Operationally expensive — often requires maintenance windows or a read-only replica architecture.

Cross-shard queries & scatter-gather: Queries spanning multiple shards follow the scatter-gather pattern: (1) Fan out query to all N shards in parallel, (2) Each shard returns partial results, (3) Coordinator merges, sorts, and returns. Problem: Query latency = max(shard latency). ORDER BY + LIMIT requires merging at the coordinator. Mitigations: (1) Design schema to make cross-shard queries rare. (2) Use local secondary indexes per shard. (3) Pre-compute materialized views per shard.

sections.title: Consensus Algorithms — Paxos, Raft, Zab, and FLP Impossibility
sections.description:
Consensus algorithms allow distributed systems to agree on a single value despite node failures, network delays, and message loss. This is the foundation of fault-tolerant coordination.

Why consensus matters: Without consensus, a primary-backup database can experience split-brain — two nodes both believe they are leader, leading to concurrent writes and data corruption. Consensus ensures exactly one leader and exactly one committed value.

Paxos (Lamport, 1998): Three roles — Proposers (suggest values), Acceptors (vote), Learners (learn the decided value). Two phases: Prepare (proposer sends prepare(n), acceptors promise not to accept proposals < n) and Accept (proposer sends accept(n, value) if majority responds). Key guarantee: Once a value is chosen, any later proposal with a higher number must propose the same value — this is the core correctness invariant. Weakness: Notoriously hard to understand and implement. No built-in leader election or log replication.

Raft (Ongaro & Ousterhout, 2014): Designed for understandability. Decomposes consensus into three sub-problems:

  • Leader election: Nodes are followers, candidates, or leaders. Leaders send periodic heartbeats. If a follower detects no heartbeat within an election timeout (randomized 150–300ms), it becomes a candidate and starts an election. Randomized timeouts make split votes unlikely.
  • Log replication: Leader receives client requests, appends them to its log, replicates via AppendEntries RPCs. An entry is committed when replicated to a majority. Leader determines safe application to state machine.
  • Safety guarantees: (1) At most one leader per term. (2) Leader never overwrites its log. (3) If two logs match at index+term, all prior entries match. (4) Committed entries survive leader changes. (5) Servers apply log entries in order.
  • Used by: Etcd (Kubernetes), Consul (Hashicorp), TiDB, CockroachDB, MongoDB (config servers), RethinkDB.

ZooKeeper's Zab protocol: Zookeeper Atomic Broadcast (similar to Raft, predates it). Leader handles writes; followers serve reads (stale possible). Three phases: Discovery (new leader collects committed proposals from followers), Recovery (ensures all followers have same committed state), Broadcast (normal ordered delivery). Zab guarantees total order broadcast, not just state machine replication.

FLP Impossibility (Fischer, Lynch, Paterson, 1985): In an asynchronous distributed system (no bounds on message delay or processing time), no consensus protocol can guarantee progress with even a single faulty process. Practical impact: Paxos and Raft use timeouts (failure detectors) to achieve liveness — they assume a partially synchronous model (eventual bounds on message delay). The FLP result means you cannot have both safety and liveness in a fully asynchronous system. All practical consensus algorithms are safe but only eventually live under timing assumptions.

sections.title: Load Balancing — Algorithms, Layers, and Global Routing
sections.description:
Load balancers distribute incoming traffic across backend servers. FAANG interviews expect you to understand the full stack from DNS to L7 routing.

Algorithms:

  • Round robin: Rotate through servers. Simple, no state. Problem: ignores server load — a slow server accumulates backlog. Best for homogeneous backends.
  • Least connections: Send to server with fewest active connections. Handles variable request processing time. Problem: long-lived connections (WebSockets) skew the count.
  • IP hash: hash(client_ip) % N ensures session affinity. Problem: remaps on server add/remove (consistent hashing fixes this).
  • Consistent hashing-based: Minimal remapping on server change. Used by: HAProxy (hash-type consistent), NGINX (hash $request_id consistent).
  • Weighted variants: Assign weights to backends (weight=3 for 32-core, weight=1 for 8-core). Works with any algorithm above.

Layer 4 vs Layer 7:

  • L4 (Transport layer): Routes on IP + port. No visibility into HTTP headers or cookies. Faster, lower overhead. Uses NAT or DSR. Examples: AWS NLB, HAProxy (TCP mode), IPVS.
  • L7 (Application layer): Routes on HTTP headers, URL path, cookies, body. Can terminate TLS, inspect traffic, rewrite URLs. Examples: AWS ALB, NGINX, Envoy, HAProxy (HTTP mode), Google HTTP(S) LB.
  • Tradeoff: L4 is faster, supports any TCP protocol. L7 is feature-rich but adds latency (TLS termination, header parsing). Common pattern: L4 LB in front of L7 LBs (NLB → NGINX → app servers).

Health checks & circuit breakers:

  • Passive: LB monitors client errors (5xx). If a server exceeds threshold, removed from rotation. Simple, but degrades UX during detection window.
  • Active: LB periodically probes GET /health. More proactive but adds probing traffic.
  • Circuit breakers (Hystrix, Resilience4j): Client-side pattern: track error rate per backend. If errors exceed threshold, open the circuit — all requests fail fast. After cooldown, half-open state allows trial requests. Prevents cascading failures — a slow backend doesn't cause all clients to exhaust resources.

Global vs Local Load Balancing:

  • Local LB: Distributes traffic within a DC/region. Typically L4 or L7. Example: ALB distributing across EC2 instances in us-east-1.
  • Global LB (across regions): DNS-based. (1) GeoDNS — route based on requester location (MaxMind GeoIP). (2) Latency-based routing — AWS Route53 probes endpoint latency, returns lowest-latency IP. (3) Weighted round robin — shift % traffic between regions for canary deployments. (4) Anycast — same IP advertised from multiple regions; BGP routes to nearest. Used by CloudFlare, Google, AWS Route53.

sections.title: CQRS & Event Sourcing — Command Query Responsibility Segregation
sections.description:
CQRS separates read and write operations into different models. Event sourcing stores state changes as an append-only event log. They are often used together but are independent patterns.

CQRS Basics: Traditional CRUD uses the same model for reads and writes. CQRS separates them:

  • Command model (write side): Handles commands (actions like "PlaceOrder"). Commands are imperative, validated before execution. Return no data (just success/failure). Optimized for write consistency.
  • Query model (read side): Handles queries (reads like "getOrderHistory"). Can use different data stores — denormalized tables, materialized views, even different DB types. Optimized for read performance.

Why separate models? In complex domains, read and write shapes differ fundamentally. A transactional write needs normalized data (avoid anomalies). A read needs denormalized data (avoid joins). With CQRS, use Postgres for writes and Elasticsearch for reads — each tuned for its workload.

Event Sourcing: Instead of storing current state, store a sequence of events that led to it. Current state is derived by replaying all events. Example: a bank account stores "AccountCreated(500)", "Withdrawn(200)", "Deposited(50)" — current balance = 500 - 200 + 50 = 350.

  • Benefits: Complete audit trail, time travel (reconstruct state at any point), temporal queries, event-driven fan-out to multiple consumers (read model, analytics, ML pipeline).
  • Challenges: Event schema evolution (events are immutable), replay performance (snapshotting required for large streams), eventual consistency gap, debugging complexity.

When CQRS + Event Sourcing is worth the complexity:

  • YES: Financial systems (regulatory audit trails), e-commerce (inventory + order history + analytics), collaboration tools (Google Docs revision history), compliance-sensitive systems (GDPR, HIPAA), systems with complex business rules that change over time.
  • NO: Simple CRUD apps, systems where read and write shapes are identical, small teams with tight deadlines (CQRS adds significant boilerplate), systems without audit trail requirements.

Kafka as event store: Apache Kafka is the most common event store for CQRS/Event Sourcing in production:

  • Each topic = an event type (order.created, payment.received).
  • Events are immutable, ordered, and persisted with configurable retention.
  • Log compaction retains the latest value per key — compacted topics serve as the "current state" view.
  • Kafka Streams / ksqlDB materialize read models from event streams with exactly-once semantics.
  • Kafka Connect sinks events to read stores (Elasticsearch, Postgres, S3).
  • Tradeoff: Kafka is not a database — no arbitrary point queries, no secondary indexes, no cross-topic transactions. You need a separate read store for serving queries.

Typical architecture: (1) Command service writes events to Kafka. (2) Kafka Connect or custom consumer updates the read model (denormalized Postgres tables, Elasticsearch indices). (3) Query service serves reads from the read model. (4) The read model is eventually consistent with the write model — lag is typically milliseconds to seconds. Tradeoff: You sacrifice strong consistency for read performance and service decoupling.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Designing Data-Intensive Applications (Kleppmann, 2017) — The definitive book on distributed systems design, FAANG interview essential reading
  • System Design Primer — Comprehensive collection of distributed system design patterns and FAANG case studies
  • AWS Builders' Library — Amazon's internal engineering patterns and best practices, publicly documented
  • In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (Raft) (Ongaro & Ousterhout, 2014) — The consensus algorithm used by etcd, Consul, TiKV
  • The Google File System (Ghemawat et al., 2003) — Foundation paper for distributed storage, Hadoop HDFS
  • Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store (DeCandia et al., 2007) — Consistent hashing, vector clocks, and quorum systems in production
